metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify the event-type enrollment strategy for ENQUEUE, CLAIM, and DONE: add them to `EVENT_TYPES` (required for any write), state whether they go into `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` (reducer-visible, SNAPSHOT-compacted) or are reducer-ignored like `REVIEW_RESULT` (raw-log scan-only), and note the compaction implication for whichever path is chosen.
findings.finding: The plan creates three new event types (ENQUEUE, CLAIM, DONE) but does not address enrollment in the write-path allow-list (`EVENT_TYPES` frozenset in `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py`) or resolve whether they belong in `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` (reducer-visible, compacted into SNAPSHOTs) or remain reducer-ignored like `REVIEW_RESULT` (scan-only). This design choice has material consequences: reducer-visible types fold into all tickets' compiled state and are destroyed by compaction; scan-only types require raw-log projection. Leaving this unspecified blocks correct implementation.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'New queue event types + reducer projection to pending / soaking / claimed state'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether ENQUEUE/CLAIM/DONE events belong in `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` (requiring `process_snapshot` support) or `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` (preserved-and-ignored, like `REVIEW_RESULT`), and document the compaction-safety contract for each new event type.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether ENQUEUE/CLAIM/DONE events belong in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES (reducer-dispatched, squashed into SNAPSHOT by compaction) or in _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES (preserved-and-ignored like REVIEW_RESULT). If they are added to KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES and compaction runs before the queue reducer's process_snapshot handling is implemented, queue state is silently lost.
findings.location: What / Scope sections (new queue event types + reducer projection)
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the REVIEW_RESULT pattern: add ENQUEUE/CLAIM/DONE to `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` (not `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES`) so compaction preserves their files instead of absorbing them into SNAPSHOT, avoiding any need for `process_snapshot` queue reconstruction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ENQUEUE, CLAIM, and DONE to `event_append.EVENT_TYPES` (write allow-list) and to `reducer/_version.py:_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` (fsck suppress), and confirm they are absent from `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` (so the main reducer ignores them and compaction skips them — matching the REVIEW_RESULT pattern).
findings.finding: The plan introduces three new event types (ENQUEUE, CLAIM, DONE) but does not address the two-registry constraint the codebase enforces for every new event type: (a) they must be added to `event_append.EVENT_TYPES` (the write allow-list — currently 15 types; any unlisted type raises StoreError at append time) and (b) they must be added to `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` in `reducer/_version.py` to avoid false `fsck`/`bridge_fsck` 'newer binary' warnings, while being kept OUT of `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` so the main reducer ignores them and compaction does not absorb them into SNAPSHOTs (the exact pattern used for `REVIEW_RESULT`). The plan is silent on both registries.
findings.location: What section ('ENQUEUE appended on plan-review CERTIFICATION…'; 'CLAIM = optimistic-append'); Scope ('New queue event types'); src/rebar/_store/event_append.py:EVENT_TYPES; src/rebar/reducer/_version.py:KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES + _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES
findings.suggested_fix: In `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py`, add `'ENQUEUE_ENRICHMENT'`, `'CLAIM_ENRICHMENT'`, `'DONE_ENRICHMENT'` (or your chosen names) to `EVENT_TYPES`. In `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py`, add the same names to `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES`. Do NOT add them to `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES`. Mirror the existing REVIEW_RESULT comment block for discoverability.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the new ENQUEUE, CLAIM, and DONE event types to `event_append.EVENT_TYPES` in `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` (and enumerate this file as an edit target in Scope).
findings.finding: The write-path allow-list `event_append.EVENT_TYPES` in `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` is an unenumerated consumer: adding new ENQUEUE/CLAIM/DONE event types requires extending this frozenset, or `stage_and_commit` will raise `StoreError('invalid event_type …')` for every queue write.
findings.location: Scope section / What section — 'ENQUEUE appended … CLAIM = optimistic-append'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `'ENRICHMENT_ENQUEUE'`, `'ENRICHMENT_CLAIM'`, `'ENRICHMENT_DONE'` (or whatever final names) to the `EVENT_TYPES` frozenset in `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py`, and list this file explicitly in the plan's Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py` as an edit target and specify whether the new queue event types go into `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` (reducer-dispatched, for the projection) or `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` (recognized-but-not-replayed).
findings.finding: The plan does not enumerate `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py` (`KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` / `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES`) as an edit target, yet every new event type must be classified there to avoid the forward-compat 'unknown newer type' warning path and to control whether the reducer replays or ignores the events.
findings.location: Scope section — 'New queue event types + reducer projection'
findings.suggested_fix: Add the new queue event type names to `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` (since the plan requires a reducer projection that reduces them to pending/soaking/claimed state), and list `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py` explicitly in the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the canonical string names, complete `data` field schemas, and allow-list membership (EVENT_TYPES write-path and KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES vs _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES) for all three new queue event types (ENQUEUE, CLAIM, and the done/completion event).
findings.finding: The three new event types the queue depends on (ENQUEUE, CLAIM, and the completion event named only as 'done') are referenced throughout the plan but their canonical string names, event-body (`data`) field schemas, and which allow-list they belong to (EVENT_TYPES write-path guard in `_store/event_append.py` and/or KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES replay dispatch in `reducer/_version.py`) are never defined. An implementor cannot write the ENQUEUE emitter, the reducer handler, or the `pending_enrichment` read without these definitions.
findings.location: Scope section ('New queue event types + reducer projection') and What section ('ENQUEUE appended on plan-review CERTIFICATION … CLAIM = optimistic-append')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Queue event body schemas' subsection to the Scope section specifying, for each event: its canonical type string (e.g. `ENRICHMENT_ENQUEUE`), required `data` fields (e.g. `{ticket_id, not_before_ns, enqueue_seq}`), and whether it is reducer-replayed (add to KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES + process_* handler) or reducer-ignored (add to _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES and read via a dedicated scanner like `sidecar.py`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the reaper mechanism: which process runs it, what triggers expiry detection (lease duration + clock comparison), which event it appends to expire a stale claim, and how the reducer handles that event to transition the entry back to claimable.
findings.finding: The 'reaper' for crashed-drainer lease expiry is mentioned in one clause ('a lease with a reaper so a crashed drainer's claim expires → at-least-once') but the reaper's execution context is completely unspecified: who runs it (the drain runner itself on startup? a separate process? a periodic scan?), on what trigger (wall-clock timeout? an explicit expiry event? a heartbeat absence?), which event it appends to mark expiry, and how it interacts with the optimistic-append claim protocol.
findings.location: What section ('CLAIM = optimistic-append … a lease with a reaper so a crashed drainer's claim expires') and AC 4 ('A claim whose lease expires (crashed drainer) is reclaimable')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly how 'only one claimer wins' is enforced atomically: either (a) re-read the current queue state for the ticket inside the write lock before appending the CLAIM event and abort if already claimed, or (b) document a different mechanism that provides CAS semantics within the store's flock model.
findings.finding: The 'optimistic-append, exactly one claimer wins' mechanism is unsound given the store's actual locking model. The existing event store (event_append.stage_and_commit) uses a single exclusive fcntl/mkdir flock over the whole tracker — not a compare-and-swap. Two concurrent drainers both reach the flock serially and both successfully append a CLAIM event; there is no predicate that prevents the second appender from landing. The plan's 'optimistic append' framing implies CAS semantics that do not exist in the store, leaving AC#3 ('exactly one wins') unachievable without additional mechanism.
findings.location: What section — 'CLAIM = optimistic-append (only one drainer lands; losers retry another ticket)'; AC#3
findings.suggested_fix: Under the existing store model, the drainer must perform a read-check-then-write within the same lock acquisition: acquire the write lock, scan the ticket's event files for an existing unexpired CLAIM, and only then append the CLAIM event (aborting if a concurrent CLAIM was observed). This is the check-then-act inside the critical section that gives the 'only one wins' guarantee.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'optimistic-append' description with the concrete achievable mechanism: both drainers attempt to append a CLAIM event, but each must first read the ticket's queue event log *inside* the write lock to confirm no prior CLAIM exists for that entry — if one is found, the drainer aborts and retries another ticket; clarify this is serialized-under-lock, not CAS.
findings.finding: The plan describes CLAIM as 'optimistic-append (only one drainer lands; losers retry another ticket)' implying true CAS/optimistic concurrency, but the event store uses an exclusive write lock (fcntl.flock) with no conditional-append primitive. The achievable mechanism is a pessimistic 'read-check-then-append inside the write lock', not true optimistic CAS. Describing it as optimistic-append without stating that the uniqueness check occurs inside the exclusive lock leaves the implementation with a feasibility gap and risks silent double-processing if a drainer relies on append ordering alone.
findings.location: ## What — 'CLAIM = optimistic-append (only one drainer lands; losers retry another ticket)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the ## What section, restate: 'CLAIM = serialized-check-then-append: inside the store write lock, read the ticket's queue events; if a non-expired CLAIM already exists, release the lock and retry a different entry; otherwise append the CLAIM event. This achieves mutual exclusion using the store's existing exclusive write lock rather than true CAS.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the lease duration as a concrete default in the plan (e.g. 'lease TTL = 10 minutes') so the reaper and drainer can be implemented consistently without a design round-trip.
findings.finding: The lease duration for claimed entries is never stated; the reaper cannot be implemented without knowing the expiry interval.
findings.location: What section — 'CLAIM = optimistic-append … a lease with a reaper so a crashed drainer's claim expires'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' section: 'Lease TTL = 10 minutes (configurable via queue options); the reaper considers any CLAIM older than TTL with no corresponding DONE as expired.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a testing plan covering: (a) reducer unit tests for latest-wins with burst re-certs, (b) `pending_enrichment(now)` boundary tests (at/before/after `not_before`, HLC clock), (c) concurrent-claimer test asserting exactly-one-wins, and (d) lease-expiry reclaim path with idempotency assertion.
findings.finding: No testing approach is specified for the new logic introduced: the reducer projection, `pending_enrichment(now)` soak filter, optimistic-append claim race, and lease-expiry/reclaim path. Failure paths (concurrent claimers, expired lease, append conflict) and boundary conditions (claim exactly at `not_before` boundary, HLC clock skew on soak, burst of re-certs collapsing to one entry) have no test plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs) and Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Testing' section to the plan. At minimum: unit-test the reducer with sequences of ENQUEUE events (including re-cert bursts) asserting ≤1 pending entry per ticket; property-test `pending_enrichment(now)` with time values straddling `not_before`; write a concurrent test that fires two claim attempts against the same entry and asserts exactly one succeeds; write a lease-expiry test that simulates a crashed drainer (advance clock past lease TTL) and asserts the entry becomes reclaimable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the decision to model the enrichment queue as events on the existing event store (including the latest-wins reduce contract, lease/reaper pattern, and why a broker/SQLite was rejected).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern (event store as queue substrate with latest-wins reduce, lease/reaper, optimistic-claim) but names no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc capture for this decision.
findings.location: Scope section / Why section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan what 'optimistic-append' means under the store's exclusive write lock: specify how a losing claimer detects it lost (e.g., after releasing the write lock, re-reads the projection and sees a lower-HLC CLAIM wins), and confirm the 'optimistic' characterization is accurate given the lock serializes concurrent appenders.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the event store is 'optimistically-concurrent' as a queue substrate and describes the claim as 'optimistic-append', but every event append in the codebase serializes through an exclusive write lock (`_store.lock.write_lock` in `stage_and_commit`). Two concurrent claimers therefore cannot race in a CAS/lock-free sense — they are serialized at the store level. The plan does not resolve whether 'optimistic' refers to lock-free CAS (which the store does not support) or to 'each appends a CLAIM and the reducer picks the winner by HLC order' (which is lock-serialized at write time, trivially giving one winner). This is an unverified capability assertion about the store's concurrency model.
findings.location: Why section — 'rebar's event store is already a durable, shared, optimistically-concurrent queue substrate'; What section — 'CLAIM = optimistic-append (only one drainer lands; losers retry another ticket)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note: 'Optimistic here means each drainer appends its own CLAIM event without pre-checking for a concurrent claim; after appending (and releasing the write lock), it re-reads the projection — if the winning CLAIM is not its own UUID, it backs off and retries another ticket. The write lock serializes the appends themselves; the race is resolved at reduce time by HLC ordering.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a RETIRE/compaction path for completed queue entries (e.g., snapshot-after-DONE, log pruning, or explicit event-store segment GC) so the underlying event log does not accumulate unboundedly.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no RETIRE or compaction path for DONE queue events. The event log accumulates ENQUEUE, CLAIM, and DONE events without bound; the AC5 'bounded churn' guarantee applies only to the reduced in-memory projection, not to the underlying event store growth.
findings.location: Scope section / AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a DONE event type to the reducer that marks the entry terminal, then specify a compaction rule (e.g., once a DONE event is appended for a ticket, all prior ENQUEUE/CLAIM events for that ticket are eligible for log compaction or a rolling snapshot). Document this in the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify where ENQUEUE/CLAIM/DONE events are stored (per-ticket dir, or a dedicated queue ticket/directory) and how pending_enrichment(now) reads them efficiently (e.g., a dedicated queue-dir scan, a cached cross-ticket index, or inclusion in a single queue ticket's event log).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify where ENQUEUE/CLAIM/DONE events are stored within the tracker (per-ticket directory vs. a dedicated location), nor how pending_enrichment(now) performs a cross-ticket drain read efficiently. If these events live in per-ticket directories (the natural store layout), a drain read requires scanning every ticket's directory to find unclaimed, soaked entries — equivalent to a reduce_all_tickets pass, which is expensive. The plan calls this a 'reducer projection' without addressing whether it is a per-ticket compiled-state field (requiring a full all-tickets reduce on every drain call) or a separate index.
findings.location: Scope section — 'New queue event types + reducer projection to pending/soaking/claimed state; a pending_enrichment(now) read that returns soaked, unclaimed entries'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether ENQUEUE/CLAIM/DONE events are in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES (reducer-absorbed on compaction) or reducer-ignored (requiring an explicit prune like REVIEW_RESULT's RETAIN_PER_TICKET pattern) to bound file accumulation on active tickets.
findings.finding: The plan does not address raw queue event file accumulation. The existing store has two classes: KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES (reducer-replayed, compaction-absorbed into SNAPSHOT) and reducer-ignored types like REVIEW_RESULT (compaction-preserved forever, growth bounded by an explicit prune). The new ENQUEUE/CLAIM/DONE events must fall into one of these classes, but the plan does not specify which, leaving a gap: if reducer-ignored, queue event files accumulate without bound on active tickets; if in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES, they enter compiled per-ticket state and are compaction-absorbed (but then affect the ticket's own reducer state).
findings.location: What section — 'New queue event types + reducer projection'; Scope — 'Queue churn stays bounded: latest-wins keeps the reduced pending set at ≤1 entry per ticket'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command (e.g. a runnable test invocation or CLI sequence) for each AC — at minimum: (AC1) write a cert event and assert the queue projection contains one entry with `not_before = cert_time + 3600s`; (AC2) assert `pending_enrichment(now < not_before)` returns empty and `pending_enrichment(now >= not_before)` returns the entry; (AC3) spawn two concurrent claimers and assert exactly one CLAIM event lands; (AC4) advance the clock past lease expiry and assert the entry is reclaimable; (AC5) append N ENQUEUE events for the same ticket and assert the projection contains exactly 1 pending entry.
findings.finding: No proving command is provided for any of the five ACs — an executor cannot verify done-ness without guessing what command/test to run.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all five bullets)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an end-to-end proving command for AC1 that triggers an actual plan-review certification (or a realistic integration test against the certification code path in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/`) and asserts the queue projection contains the resulting ENQUEUE event with the correct `not_before` timestamp.
findings.finding: There is no end-to-end proving command that exercises the certification→enqueue integration path (the new code path wired into `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/`).
findings.location: What section — 'Wire the ENQUEUE into the plan-review certification path (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the latest-wins reduce rule with the ENQUEUE body schema: name the exact field used as the 'latest' discriminator (e.g. the HLC event-file timestamp prefix, or a monotonic `enqueue_seq` field) and specify the tie-break.
findings.finding: The 'latest-wins reduce' rule is stated in prose ('a re-cert appends a new ENQUEUE that supersedes the prior one for that ticket') but the reduce function's keying logic — specifically, which field in the ENQUEUE event body identifies 'same ticket' and which field or property determines 'latest' (HLC timestamp of the event file? a sequence field in `data`? the `not_before` value itself?) — is not co-located with, or cross-referenced from, the ENQUEUE body schema that needs it.
findings.location: What section ('latest-wins reduce') and Scope section ('A drain-time read returns only entries whose not_before <= now and that are unclaimed')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the certification event and the ENQUEUE event are written in a single atomic batch/transaction, or if not, define a compensating mechanism (e.g., read-repair on startup, idempotent re-enqueue on re-read of unmatched cert events) to ensure AC1 holds under partial failure.
findings.finding: The plan wires ENQUEUE into the certification path as a separate append but does not specify whether the certification event and the ENQUEUE event are written atomically (single batch/transaction). A crash or error between the two appends would leave a certified ticket with no queue entry — silently never enriched.
findings.location: What section — 'Wire the ENQUEUE into the plan-review certification path'; AC1
findings.suggested_fix: If the event store supports multi-stream atomic appends, batch cert+ENQUEUE into one operation. If not, add a recovery step: on startup (or periodically) scan certified tickets lacking a corresponding ENQUEUE event and re-enqueue them, making the enqueue idempotent by keying on cert_id.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the ENQUEUE event is appended in the same atomic commit as the SIGNATURE event or in a separate commit, and if separate, specify the recovery path for a crash between the two writes.
findings.finding: The ENQUEUE wiring into the certification path has an unspecified transactional boundary: the plan says 'Wire the ENQUEUE into the plan-review certification path (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/)' but `sign_plan_review` → `signing.sign_manifest` → `stage_and_commit` is already a write-lock-held commit. The plan does not specify whether the ENQUEUE event is appended in the SAME commit (extending the lock-held window and adding queue semantics to the signing path) or as a SEPARATE subsequent commit (creating a window where the ticket is certified but not yet enqueued — violating AC 1 if the process crashes between the two commits).
findings.location: What section ('Wire the ENQUEUE into the plan-review certification path') and AC 1 ('Certifying a ticket's plan review enqueues it')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability requirements: at minimum, log each ENQUEUE/CLAIM/DONE/lease-expiry event and specify at least one metric (e.g., queue depth, expired-lease count) so operators can monitor queue health.
findings.finding: No observability is specified for queue failure points: failed ENQUEUEs, stuck/expired leases, and claim contention are new failure modes with no associated metrics, logs, or alerts.
findings.location: Scope section / What section

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the failure semantics of the ENQUEUE append on the certification path: define whether cert succeeds independently of the ENQUEUE write, what happens on ENQUEUE failure (retry, log+skip, or surfaced error), and how a missed enqueue can be recovered.
findings.finding: The plan does not describe error handling for a failed ENQUEUE append on the certification path — if the event-store write fails, it is unclear whether certification is blocked, or enrichment is silently dropped with no recovery path.
findings.location: What section: 'Wire the ENQUEUE into the plan-review certification path'; AC#1
findings.suggested_fix: Make ENQUEUE a best-effort fire-and-forget append after certification succeeds, with a logged warning on failure. A separate reconciliation pass (or re-cert) can re-enqueue missed tickets, preserving the 'never block the hot path' invariant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify the specific callsite for the ENQUEUE hook in the plan — name `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py`'s `_run_plan_review` function (inside the `sign and verdict == PASS` guard, after `attest.sign_plan_review` succeeds) as the edit target.
findings.finding: The plan names `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/` as the hook location but does not identify the specific callsite for the ENQUEUE trigger. There are two distinct code paths that reach 'certification': (a) `_run_plan_review` in `__init__.py` at the `attest.sign_plan_review` call, and (b) the workflow-ops `plan_review_coach` step (which explicitly states 'NO signing'). If the hook is placed in the wrong branch, ENQUEUE either fires on non-signing passes or never fires on the workflow path.
findings.location: What section — 'Wire the ENQUEUE into the plan-review certification path (`src/rebar/llm/plan_review/`)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope/What section, specify: 'ENQUEUE is appended inside `_run_plan_review` in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py`, immediately after a successful `attest.sign_plan_review` call, within the existing `if sign and verdict.get("verdict") == "PASS" …` guard.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Use a namespace-qualified name for the enrichment-queue operations (e.g. `ENRICHMENT_ENQUEUE`, `ENRICHMENT_CLAIM`, `ENRICHMENT_DONE`) to avoid collision with the existing ticket-claim vocabulary throughout `_commands/claim.py` and `CLAIM_RESULT`.
findings.finding: The plan uses the term 'CLAIM' for the enrichment-queue claim operation throughout, but the existing codebase already has a heavily-loaded 'claim' vocabulary (CLAIM_RESULT schema, `rebar claim` command, `_commands/claim.py`, `transition.py` CLAIMED output) all referring to ticket-claiming for work assignment. Using the same term for two unrelated operations in the same plan-review path risks instruction-locality confusion for any executor reading both the plan and the codebase simultaneously.
findings.location: What section ('CLAIM = optimistic-append') and Scope section ('claim/lease/done semantics')
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the queue event types to `ENRICHMENT_ENQUEUE`, `ENRICHMENT_CLAIM`, `ENRICHMENT_DONE` in the plan and specify these names in the event-type allow-lists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC1) that verifies timestamps on ENQUEUE events are sourced from the HLC clock (`.rebar/hlc.state`), e.g. 'the not_before value is derived from the HLC timestamp at cert time, not wall clock'.
findings.finding: The 'What' section specifies 'using the HLC clock (.rebar/hlc.state) for timestamps' as a concrete implementation constraint, but no acceptance criterion verifies that HLC — rather than wall clock — is actually used for the cert_time or not_before values. An implementer using wall clock would pass all five ACs.
findings.location: What section ('using the HLC clock (.rebar/hlc.state) for timestamps') vs. Acceptance Criteria (all five)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1: 'Certifying a ticket's plan review enqueues it with not_before = hlc_now() + 60min (HLC-sourced timestamp, not wall clock); re-certifying supersedes the prior entry and bumps not_before forward.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either derive the 60-min soak threshold from observed re-cert cadence data (spike/measurement) OR make it a named, configurable constant with a note on how to tune it — don't leave it as a magic number.
findings.finding: The 60-minute soak/debounce threshold is asserted in the spec with no measurement plan, data reference, or configurability — yet soak duration is explicitly described as a debounce parameter likely to require tuning as usage patterns emerge.
findings.location: What section / AC#1: 'not_before = cert_time + 60min'
findings.suggested_fix: Extract to a named config constant (e.g., `ENRICHMENT_SOAK_MINUTES = 60`) with a comment citing the rationale and how to adjust. Add a 'TBD: validate against observed re-cert burst patterns' note if no data yet exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split AC4 into two parts: (a) 'A claim whose lease expires is reclaimable by a subsequent claimer' (in-scope, verifiable here) and (b) 'enrichment idempotency is verified in S4' (explicitly deferred with rationale), so the in-scope AC is evaluable without the drain runner.
findings.finding: AC4 claims 're-processing is safe (idempotent overwrite)' but enrichment idempotency is explicitly out of scope (S4/drain runner), making this criterion unverifiable within this ticket's deliverable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: 'A claim whose lease expires (crashed drainer) is reclaimable; re-processing is safe (idempotent overwrite).'
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC4 to: 'A claim whose lease expires is reclaimable: a subsequent CLAIM event for the same ticket is accepted by the event store after TTL elapses. (Processing-safety / idempotency is verified in S4 where the enrichment action is built.)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback section that asserts the post-rollback on-disk schema state (e.g., 'ENQUEUE/CLAIM/DONE files remain on disk and are treated as forward-compat preserved-and-ignored payload by older rebar; no data loss, no cleanup required') and note any compensating-forward steps if cleanup is needed.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the post-rollback schema state. Once ENQUEUE/CLAIM/DONE events are written to disk, rolling back the feature code leaves those files on disk. The plan never describes what state the event store is in after rollback, nor whether a compensating-forward cleanup is needed.
findings.location: Scope section / Acceptance Criteria — no rollback path stated

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC with an observable, end-to-end validation mechanism — e.g., 'certifying a plan review does not block the write-path response (measured by unchanged p99 latency)' or 'a certified ticket appears in the enrichment queue within one HLC tick as confirmed by a queue-state read'.
findings.finding: No AC carries a concrete validation mechanism (before/after workflow, operational metric, or dogfooding signal) that would let a reviewer observe an improvement to the enrichment pipeline. All five ACs are pure system-internal correctness properties with no user-observable outcome.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate the existing `_ref_lock` CAS lease mechanism as prior art for the CLAIM/lease/reaper component, and document in the plan why per-ticket-event claim is preferred over (or incompatible with) the existing git-ref-based lease.
findings.finding: The plan proposes CLAIM/DONE events with lease/reaper semantics but does not consider the codebase's existing distributed CAS lease mechanism (the reconciler ref-lock in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_ref_lock.py`), which already implements acquire/release/renew/steal over git refs with heartbeat-based lease expiry. No justification is given for why this mature prior art cannot serve the claim/lease/reaper requirement, or why the novel per-ticket-event approach is preferred.
findings.location: What section — 'CLAIM = optimistic-append ... + a lease with a reaper so a crashed drainer's claim expires'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
